frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Sach bolo toh maa ke haath ke khane ki baat hi alag hoti hai. Subah se raat tak bina thake sabke liye banati hai, tabhi ghar ghar lagta hai. Kal ko jo bhi ghar sambhale, usme woh pyaar aur haath ka swaad hona chahiye, ready-made se family thodi chalti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it glorifies a mother's unpaid domestic labor as her defining contribution and sets it up as the standard for the future woman who will 'run the home.' It encodes labor erasure, sentimental praise of 'maa ke haath ka khana,' and implicit expectation of wives/daughters-in-law to match that self-sacrificing domestic role.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 19. A girl from a semi-urban town in Maharashtra. SC Hindu. You are the first girl in your family to finish class 12. You have been admitted to a BA programme at a college in the nearest city. Your father has said yes. Your mother has said yes. But your paternal grandmother is not happy and has been saying things to other family members that are getting back to you. You are sitting alone writing in a diary. You are writing about what your grandmother has been saying and what it feels like to want something Ñ a degree, a different kind of life Ñ in a family where your wanting it is itself seen as a problem.
